symbols: X-shaped cross (saltire) — the specific instrument of Andrew's martyrdom, his unique attribute
saint embracing the cross — willing acceptance of martyrdom for Christ's sake
angel bearing crown and palm — the martyr's heavenly reward of victory and glory
upward ecstatic gaze — the beatific vision glimpsed in the hour of death
divine light from above — God's approval and the heavenly glory descending upon the martyr
bare, aged body — the vulnerability and humanity of the saint in his final hour
description: A dramatic vertical composition showing an elderly bearded man with bare chest and flowing blue-gold drapery, embracing a large wooden X-shaped cross with one arm while extending the other arm upward in ecstasy. He gazes heavenward with an expression of rapture. Above, a small angel (putto) holds a laurel crown and a palm branch, descending from a burst of divine light that breaks through dramatic dark clouds. The landscape behind is moody with mountains and atmospheric sky. The entire scene conveys willing embrace of sacrificial death with the assurance of heavenly reward.
theological_description: Saint Andrew, Simon Peter's brother and one of Christ's first disciples (John 1:40-42), was according to tradition crucified on an X-shaped cross (crux decussata) in Patras, Greece. Rather than shrink from his execution, Andrew is depicted embracing his cross with joy — an embodiment of Jesus's command: 'Whoever wants to be my disciple must deny themselves and take up their cross and follow me' (Matthew 16:24). Tradition records that Andrew, upon seeing the cross prepared for him, exclaimed: 'O good cross, long desired, now prepared for me!' — transforming the instrument of shameful death into an object of love. The angel descending with crown and palm fulfills the promise of Revelation 2:10: 'Be faithful unto death, and I will give you the crown of life.' Andrew's ecstatic upward gaze mirrors Stephen's vision at his own martyrdom (Acts 7:55-56), suggesting that in the moment of death, the martyr is granted a vision of heavenly glory. As one of the first called and the one who brought his brother Peter to Jesus, Andrew's faithful death completes the arc of discipleship — from first following to final witness.

scripture_refs.reason: Andrew as one of the first disciples called, who brought his brother Peter to Jesus
Take up your cross and follow me — Andrew literally embracing his cross fulfills Christ's command
Be faithful unto death and I will give you the crown of life — the angel descending with the martyr's crown
Stephen's heavenly vision at his martyrdom — Andrew's ecstatic upward gaze parallels this
For me to live is Christ and to die is gain — the joyful embrace of martyrdom
